quiz_questions.question_text: What is a force?

options.text: The speed at which an object moves
A push or pull that can change an object's motion, shape, or direction
The mass of an object
A type of energy stored in an object

quiz_questions.explanation: A force is an interaction that, when unbalanced, causes a change in an object's velocity (acceleration). Forces can also deform objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of force?

options.text: Newton (N)
Joule (J)
Kilogram (kg)
Meter per second (m/s)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI unit of force is the newton (N), defined as 1 N = 1 kg·m/s². It is named after Sir Isaac Newton.

quiz_questions.question_text: Forces always occur in what type of arrangement?

options.text: Isolation — forces act independently
Random groupings
Triplets — every force produces two reactions
Pairs — every force has an equal and opposite reaction force

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's third law states that forces always come in interaction pairs. When object A pushes on object B, B pushes back on A with equal magnitude and opposite direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a contact force?

options.text: Only the gravitational force
A force that only exists in a vacuum
A force that requires physical touching between objects
A force that acts through empty space

quiz_questions.explanation: Contact forces require direct physical contact between the interacting objects. Examples include friction, normal force, tension, and applied forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-contact force?

options.text: A force that acts on an object without physical contact, through a field
A force that only acts when objects touch
A force that has no effect on motion
A force that exists only in theory

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-contact (field) forces act at a distance through fields. Examples include gravitational, electromagnetic, and nuclear forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is an example of a non-contact force?

options.text: Gravity
Tension
Normal force
Friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity acts between any two masses without requiring contact — it operates through the gravitational field. Friction, tension, and normal force all require contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a net force?

options.text: Any single force applied to an object
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object
The largest force acting on an object
The force of gravity on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: The net force (resultant force) is the vector sum of all individual forces. An object accelerates only when the net force is nonzero.

quiz_questions.question_text: When the net force on an object is zero, the object is said to be in:

options.text: Free fall
Maximum acceleration
Terminal velocity
Equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: When the net force is zero, the object is in equilibrium — it either remains at rest or moves at constant velocity (Newton's first law).

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to an object when an unbalanced (net) force acts on it?

options.text: It moves at constant velocity
It remains at rest
It accelerates in the direction of the net force
It immediately stops

quiz_questions.explanation: An unbalanced net force causes acceleration in the direction of that force (Newton's second law: F_net = ma).

quiz_questions.question_text: Is force a scalar or vector quantity?

options.text: Scalar — it has only magnitude
Vector — it has both magnitude and direction
Neither — it is dimensionless
It depends on the type of force

quiz_questions.explanation: Force is a vector quantity. Both the magnitude (how strong) and the direction (which way) of a force are essential for describing its effect on motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a force?

options.text: The energy an object possesses due to its position
A measure of how fast an object is moving
The motion of an object through space
An interaction between two objects that can cause acceleration, deformation, or a change in direction — characterized by magnitude, direction, and point of application

quiz_questions.explanation: A force is a vector interaction between two objects. It is fully described by its magnitude (size), direction, and point of application on the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a contact force?

options.text: A force that acts between objects regardless of distance
A force produced by electric charges at a distance
A force that arises from the direct physical interaction between two objects that are touching
The gravitational attraction between two objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Contact forces result from the electromagnetic interactions at the surfaces where objects meet. They include normal force, friction, tension, air resistance, and applied forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a field force?

options.text: A mathematical concept with no real-world effects
A force that acts on an object through a field, without requiring physical contact between the interacting objects
A force that acts only on the surface of objects
A force exerted only by magnets

quiz_questions.explanation: Field forces (non-contact forces) are transmitted through fields that permeate space. Gravitational, electric, and magnetic forces are all field forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes net force?

options.text: The single resultant force obtained by taking the vector sum of all individual forces acting on an object
The force applied by the hand only
The strongest individual force on an object
The average of all forces on an object

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = ΣF = F₁ + F₂ + F₃ + ... (vector sum). It determines the acceleration of the object: a = F_net/m.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes equilibrium?

options.text: A state where the object is perfectly still
A state in which the net force on an object is zero, resulting in no acceleration — the object either remains at rest or moves at constant velocity
A state achieved only in zero gravity
A state where all forces are equal in magnitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium (ΣF = 0) means no acceleration. Static equilibrium: object at rest. Dynamic equilibrium: object moves at constant velocity. In both cases, net force is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a free-body diagram?

options.text: A graph of force versus time
A simplified drawing that shows an object isolated from its surroundings with all external forces acting on it represented as labeled arrows
A diagram of an object falling freely
A sketch of an experiment setup

quiz_questions.explanation: A free-body diagram (FBD) isolates an object and shows every external force as a vector arrow originating from the object's center. It is the primary tool for force analysis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a fundamental force?

options.text: Any force measured in newtons
The force needed to move a 1 kg mass
A force that only acts on fundamental particles
One of the four basic forces of nature — gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear — from which all other forces are derived

quiz_questions.explanation: The four fundamental forces are: gravitational (between masses), electromagnetic (between charges), strong nuclear (binds quarks/nucleons), and weak nuclear (responsible for radioactive decay).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the normal force?

options.text: The friction force between two surfaces
The force of gravity on an object
The perpendicular contact force exerted by a surface on an object resting on or pressing against it, preventing the object from passing through the surface
A force that is always equal to the object's weight

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force acts perpendicular to the contact surface. On a horizontal surface it often equals the object's weight, but on inclines or when additional vertical forces act, it differs.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tension?

options.text: The gravitational force between two objects
The force of a spring when compressed
The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, cable, or similar object when it is pulled taut by forces acting at each end
A force that pushes objects apart

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension is a pulling force along the length of a flexible connector. In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an interaction pair?

options.text: Two identical forces acting in the same direction
Two forces acting on the same object in opposite directions
Two forces of equal magnitude and opposite direction that act on two different objects as described by Newton's third law
A pair of objects that never interact

quiz_questions.explanation: An interaction pair (action-reaction pair) consists of two forces: F_AB on B by A, and F_BA on A by B. They are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and act on different objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: A book rests on a table. Identify all forces acting on the book.

options.text: Only the normal force acts on the book
Weight (gravitational force downward) and normal force from the table (upward) — these two forces are balanced, keeping the book in equilibrium
Friction and tension
Only gravity acts on the book

quiz_questions.explanation: Two forces act on the book: (1) Weight (mg downward) from Earth's gravitational pull, and (2) Normal force (N upward) from the table surface. Since the book is at rest, N = mg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pushes a box across the floor at constant velocity by applying 50 N of force. What can you conclude about friction?

options.text: Friction is zero because the box is moving
Friction is greater than 50 N
Friction equals 50 N — since velocity is constant, net force is zero, so friction must exactly balance the applied 50 N push
Friction is less than 50 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, which means ΣF = 0. Therefore friction = applied force = 50 N. The forces are balanced.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two teams play tug-of-war. Team A pulls with 500 N and Team B pulls with 450 N. What is the net force and which direction does the rope accelerate?

options.text: 0 N — the forces cancel
950 N total
50 N toward Team B
50 N toward Team A — net force = 500 − 450 = 50 N in Team A's direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force = 500 − 450 = 50 N toward Team A (the stronger puller). The rope (and both teams) will accelerate in Team A's direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: An apple hangs from a tree branch by its stem. Draw the free-body diagram mentally. How many forces act on the apple?

options.text: One — only gravity
Zero — it is in equilibrium so no forces act
Three — gravity, tension, and normal force
Two — weight (gravity pulling down) and tension in the stem (pulling up)

quiz_questions.explanation: The apple has weight (mg downward) and tension in the stem (T upward). Since the apple is in equilibrium: T = mg. There is no normal force since the apple hangs freely.

quiz_questions.question_text: You stand on a bathroom scale in an elevator. The scale reads your normal weight. What can you say about the elevator's motion?

options.text: The elevator must be stationary
The elevator is either at rest or moving at constant velocity — zero acceleration means the scale reads your true weight
The elevator is in free fall
The elevator must be accelerating upward

quiz_questions.explanation: A scale measures normal force. When N = mg (your true weight), the net force is zero, meaning the elevator has zero acceleration — it could be at rest or moving at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person pushes two stacked boxes (10 kg and 5 kg) with 30 N of force on a frictionless surface. What is the acceleration of the system?

options.text: 6 m/s²
3 m/s²
0.5 m/s²
2 m/s² — a = F/m_total = 30/15 = 2 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: The total mass of the system is 10 + 5 = 15 kg. Since the surface is frictionless: a = F_net/m = 30/15 = 2 m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: A parachutist falls at constant velocity. Her weight is 700 N. What is the upward drag force?

options.text: 0 N — she is falling
700 N — at constant velocity (terminal velocity), drag force equals weight so net force is zero

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration (ΣF = 0). Therefore drag force upward must equal weight downward: F_drag = 700 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: Three forces act on an object: 10 N east, 6 N west, and 8 N north. What is the magnitude of the net force?

options.text: 4 N
8 N
√(4² + 8²) = √80 ≈ 8.94 N — horizontal net = 10 − 6 = 4 N east; vertical = 8 N north; resultant = √(16 + 64)

quiz_questions.explanation: Horizontal: 10 − 6 = 4 N east. Vertical: 8 N north. Net force = √(4² + 8²) = √80 ≈ 8.94 N at arctan(8/4) ≈ 63.4° north of east.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spacecraft far from any planet drifts at 100 m/s with no forces acting on it. A student says it will eventually slow down. Is the student correct?

options.text: Yes — all objects eventually stop
Yes — space has friction that slows objects
No — it will actually speed up
No — with no net force, the spacecraft maintains constant velocity indefinitely (Newton's first law applies in the zero-force environment of deep space)

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's first law: without a net force, an object's velocity remains unchanged. In deep space, with negligible gravitational and drag forces, the spacecraft continues at 100 m/s forever.

quiz_questions.question_text: You are in a car that suddenly brakes. You feel pushed forward. Is there a real forward force on you?

options.text: Yes — the dashboard exerts a forward force
Yes — inertia is a force that pushes you forward
No — you only feel the seatbelt
No — your body tends to continue forward at the car's original speed (inertia); the sensation of being pushed forward is due to the car decelerating beneath you, not a real forward force

quiz_questions.explanation: When the car brakes, there is no forward force on you. Your body simply continues at the original velocity due to inertia (Newton's first law), while the car slows. The seatbelt provides the true backward force that decelerates you.
